analogy: Think of "Environment and Society" through the chapter's own opening images: 50 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY CHAPTER 3 ENVIRONMENT AND SOCIETY Look around you. If you are in a classroom, you may see students in uniform, sitting on chairs with books open on their desk. There are school bags with lunch and pencil boxes. Have you ever thought... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Environment and Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 50 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY CHAPTER 3 ENVIRONMENT AND SOCIETY Look around you.

The text explains that if you are in a classroom, you may see students in uniform, sitting on chairs with books open on their desk.

The text explains that there are school bags with lunch and pencil boxes.

The text explains that have you ever thought about where these things — school clothes, furniture, bags, electricity, come from?

The text explains that if you trace their origins, you will find that the source of each material object lies in nature.

The text explains that every day, we use objects whose production draws upon natural resources from around the world.

The text explains that the chair in your classroom may be made from wood with iron nails, glue and varnish.

The text explains that its journey from a tree in a forest or plantation to you depends on electricity, diesel, facilities for trade, and telecommunications.

The text explains that along the way, it has passed through the hands of loggers, carpenters, supervisors and managers, transporters, traders and those in charge of buying school furniture.

The text explains that these producers and distributors, and the inputs that they provide into chair manufacturing, in turn use a variety of goods and services derived from nature.

The text explains that try and map these resource flows and you will soon see how complex such relationships are!

The text explains that in this chapter, we will study social relationships with the environment as they have changed over time and as they vary from place to place.

The text explains that it is important to analyse and interpret such variations in a systematic way.

The text explains that there are many urgent environmental problems that demand our attention.

The text explains that to address these crises effectively, we need a sociological framework for understanding why they occur and how they might be prevented or resolved.

The text explains that the term ecology denotes the web of physical and biological systems and processes of which humans are one element.

The text explains that for example, the plant and animal life unique to a desert is adapted to its scarce rainfall, rocky or sandy soils, and extreme temperatures.

The text explains that the built environment of a city, made from concrete, cement, brick, stone, glass and tar, uses natural resources but is very much a human artefact.

The text explains that pr operty relations determine how and by whom natural resources can be used.

The text explains that private ownership of land and water sources will affect whether others can have access to these resources and on what terms and conditions.

The text explains that ownership and control over resources is also related to the division of labour in the production process.

The text explains that landless labourers and women will have a different relationship with natural resources than men.

The text explains that in rural India, women are likely to experience resource scarcity more acutely because gathering fuel and fetching water are generally women’s tasks but they do not control these resources.

The text explains that colonialism generated a g reat deal of knowledge about envir onment and society, often systematically compiling it in order to make resources available to the imperial powers.

The text explains that geology, geography, botany, zoology, for estry and hydraulic engineering were among the many disciplines that were created and institutionalised to facilitate the management of natural resources for colonial purposes.

The text explains that with the spread of indu- strialisation, resource extraction has expanded and accelerated, affecting ecosystems in unprecedented ways.

The text explains that resource Depletion Using up non-renewable natural resources is one of the most serious environmental problems.

The text explains that this agricultural resource, too, is being destroyed due to poor environmental management leading to erosion, water-logging and salinisation.

The text explains that biodiversity habitats such as forests, grasslands and wetlands are the other major resource facing rapid depletion, largely due to the expansion of areas under agriculture.

The text explains that the sources of air pollution include emissions from industries and vehicles, as well as the burning of wood and coal for domestic use.

The text explains that but we often don’t realise that indoor pollution from cooking fires is also a serious source of risk.

The text explains that major sources include not only domestic sewage and factory effluents but also the runoff from farms where large amounts of synthetic * Weblink: www.who.int/mediacentre/news/releases/2014/air-pollution/en/

The text explains that sources include amplified loudspeakers used at religious and cultural events, political campaigns, vehicle horns and traffic, and construction work.

The text explains that this has caused a small but significant rise in global temperatures.

The text explains that to make this point more concrete: economic, ethnic, cultural, and gender conflicts, among many others, lie at the core of the most serious ecological dislocations we face today — apart, to be sure, from those that are produced by natural catastrophes. – Murray Bookchin, political philosopher and founder of the Institute for Social Ecology Two examples of environment- society conflicts are given below: Sustainable Development The relation between ecology and economy has been a complex one.

The text explains that there is growing concern worldwide that if the present pace of depletion of natural resource and extinction of biodiversity continues for some more time, the future generation will have to pay the price for it. “Sustainable development is development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

The text explains that it is about the survival of the fittest, in a way that would have made Darwin insane.” (Why shouldn’t I be intolerant?, Sunita Narain in Down to Earth, 25 January 2016) We are living in an unequal world where we want to control resources and opportunities.

The text explains that the already existing system of social stratification makes it only too easy for some sections of people to control most of the available resources and opportunities.

The text explains that we need to build a society where people are at par; where there is equitable distribution of resources; where the aim is development but one that is inclusive and not exclusive.

The text explains that besides land, air and water have also become highly contested resources in the urban environment.

The text explains that gLOSSARY Hydrology: The science of water and its flows; or the broad structure of water resources in a country or region.

The text explains that green House: A covered structure for protecting plants from extremes of climate, usually from excessive cold; a green house (also called a hot house) maintains a warmer temperature inside compared to the outside temperature.

The text explains that what are the major environmental issues associated with resource depletion?

The text explains that describe some environment related conflicts that you know of or have read about.

The text explains that (Other than the examples in the text.) REFERENCES Centre for Science and Environment.

The text explains that the State of India’s Environment: The Citizens’ Report.

The text explains that 2004. ‘Planet of Slums: Urban Involution and the Informal Proletariat’ in New Left Review, 26: 5-34.

The text explains that 2004. ‘The Political Ecology of Famine: The Origins of the Third World’ in Richard Peet and Michael Watts (eds) Liberation Ecologies: Environment, Development, Social Movements.

The text explains that ecology and Equity: The Use and Abuse of Nature in Contemporary India.

The text explains that 1997. ‘The Environmentalism of the Poor’ in Ramachandra Guha and J.

The text explains that varieties of Environmentalism: Essays North and South.

The text explains that available at: http://www.downtoearth.org.in (accessed on 25 January 2016) POLLAN, MICHAEL.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society, Chapter 3: Environment and Society

revision_bullets: 50 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY CHAPTER 3 ENVIRONMENT AND SOCIETY Look around you. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)
If you are in a classroom, you may see students in uniform, sitting on chairs with books open on their desk. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)
Have you ever thought about where these things — school clothes, furniture, bags, electricity, come from? (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)
If you trace their origins, you will find that the source of each material object lies in nature. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)
Every day, we use objects whose production draws upon natural resources from around the world. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)
The chair in your classroom may be made from wood with iron nails, glue and varnish. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)
Its journey from a tree in a forest or plantation to you depends on electricity, diesel, facilities for trade, and telecommunications. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)

Along the way, it has passed through the hands of loggers, carpenters, supervisors and managers, transporters, traders and those in charge of buying school furniture. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)

These producers and distributors, and the inputs that they provide into chair manufacturing, in turn use a variety of goods and services derived from nature. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)
Try and map these resource flows and you will soon see how complex such relationships are! (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: Its
cases.point: Its dominant tree species is Prosopis juliflora (vilayati kikar or vilayati babul) which was introduced into India from South America and which has become naturalised all... (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: Did
cases.point: Did you know that the chaurs, the wide grassy meadows of Corbett National Park in Uttarakhand which offer excellent views of wildlife, were once agricultural fields? (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)

cases.name: ENVIRONMENT AND SOCIETY The
cases.point: 53 ENVIRONMENT AND SOCIETY The ecological effects of the Industrial Revolution in Britain were felt all over the world. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 3)

cases.name: Large
cases.point: Large areas of southern North America and the Caribbean were converted to plantations to meet the demand for cotton in the mills of Lancashire. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 3)

cases.name: Young West Africans
cases.point: Young West Africans were forcibly transported to America to work as slave labour on the plantations. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 3)

schemes.name: On
schemes.point: On the other hand, the social organisation of capitalism has shaped nature across the world. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 1)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The interaction between environment and society is shaped by social organisation. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 3)

schemes.name: For
schemes.point: For instance, if forests are owned by the government, it will have the power to decide whether it should lease them to timber companies or allow... (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 3)

schemes.name: Social
schemes.point: Social organisation influences how different social groups relate to their environment. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 3)

schemes.name: Complex
schemes.point: Complex industrial technologies and modes of organisation require sophisticated management systems which are often fragile and vulnerable to error. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch3 pg 4)

ncert_coverage: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Chapter 3: Environment and Society
prelims_traps: Do not treat 50 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY and CHAPTER 3 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Environment and Society; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Environment and Society through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 50 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY and CHAPTER 3 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Environment and Society, which statement is supported by the source text about 52 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY?
options: The ecology of a place is also affected by the interaction between its geography and hydrology.
For example, the plant and animal life unique to a desert is adapted to its scarce rainfall, rocky or sandy soils, and extreme temperatures.
51 ENVIRONMENT AND SOCIETY ecological factors limit and shape how human beings can live in any particular place.
Over time, however, ecology has been modified by human action.
